sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Imagine your mobile app calls five backend endpoints.
Without a gateway, every backend team solves the same problems again:
→ auth token validation
→ throttling
→ request logging
→ CORS
→ versioning
→ partner access control

That creates duplicated logic and inconsistent security.

API Gateway centralizes those cross-cutting concerns.

MENTAL MODEL:
API Gateway is the policy layer in front of your application.
Your backend should answer:
→ what business logic should happen?

The gateway should answer:
→ who can call this?
→ how much traffic is allowed?
→ what shape should request/response take?
→ should this response be cached?
```

---

MENTAL MODEL:

→ what business logic should happen?

The gateway should answer:

→ what shape should request/response take?
→ should this response be cached?

sections.title: 🚪 What is API Gateway?
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
API Gateway = fully managed service to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs

WITHOUT API GATEWAY:
Your backend (Lambda/ECS) must handle:
→ SSL termination
→ Authentication (validate JWT on every request)
→ Rate limiting (count requests per user per second)
→ Request/response transformation
→ Caching (avoid hitting backend for same data)
→ API versioning (/v1, /v2)
→ Usage plans and API keys for partners
→ Logging every request

WITH API GATEWAY:
All of the above handled at the gateway layer.
Your backend just handles business logic.
```

---

blocks.content:
API Gateway = fully managed service to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs

WITHOUT API GATEWAY:
Your backend (Lambda/ECS) must handle:

WITH API GATEWAY:
All of the above handled at the gateway layer.
Your backend just handles business logic.

revisionPoints: → Rate limiting (count requests per user per second)
→ Caching (avoid hitting backend for same data)
→ Usage plans and API keys for partners

sections.title: 🔧 API Gateway Types
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
REST API:
→ Full-featured: auth, throttling, caching, transforms, usage plans
→ More expensive: $3.50/million requests
→ Regional or edge-optimized (CloudFront in front)
→ Use for: most REST APIs, when you need advanced features

HTTP API:
→ Simpler and cheaper: $1.00/million requests
→ Lower latency than REST API
→ Limited features: no API keys, no caching, fewer auth options
→ Supports: JWT auth, Lambda, HTTP backends, CORS
→ Use for: simple Lambda or HTTP backend integrations, cost-sensitive

WebSocket API:
→ Persistent bidirectional connections
→ Real-time applications: chat, live updates, gaming
→ AWS manages connection state (connection IDs in DynamoDB)
→ Use for: real-time features

CHOOSE:
Need full features → REST API
Simple Lambda/HTTP backend, want cheapest → HTTP API
Real-time bidirectional → WebSocket API
```

---

blocks.content:
REST API:
→ Full-featured: auth, throttling, caching, transforms, usage plans
→ More expensive: $3.50/million requests
→ Regional or edge-optimized (CloudFront in front)
→ Use for: most REST APIs, when you need advanced features

HTTP API:
→ Simpler and cheaper: $1.00/million requests
→ Lower latency than REST API
→ Limited features: no API keys, no caching, fewer auth options
→ Supports: JWT auth, Lambda, HTTP backends, CORS
→ Use for: simple Lambda or HTTP backend integrations, cost-sensitive

WebSocket API:
→ Persistent bidirectional connections
→ Real-time applications: chat, live updates, gaming
→ AWS manages connection state (connection IDs in DynamoDB)
→ Use for: real-time features

CHOOSE:
Need full features → REST API
Simple Lambda/HTTP backend, want cheapest → HTTP API
Real-time bidirectional → WebSocket API

revisionPoints: → Full-featured: auth, throttling, caching, transforms, usage plans
→ Use for: most REST APIs, when you need advanced features
→ Limited features: no API keys, no caching, fewer auth options
→ Supports: JWT auth, Lambda, HTTP backends, CORS

sections.title: 🔑 Key Features
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Authentication Options
```
LAMBDA AUTHORIZER (custom auth):
→ Your Lambda validates the token (JWT, OAuth, API key, custom)
→ Returns: IAM policy (allow/deny) + principal + context
→ Result cached (TTL configurable) → don't call Lambda every request
→ Use for: custom auth logic, third-party auth providers

COGNITO USER POOLS:
→ Users authenticate with Cognito → get JWT token
→ API Gateway validates JWT automatically (no custom code)
→ No Lambda needed for validation
→ Use for: end-user authentication

IAM AUTHORIZATION:
→ Caller signs request with AWS Signature V4
→ API Gateway validates signature + checks IAM permissions
→ Use for: service-to-service calls, AWS SDK clients

API KEYS + USAGE PLANS:
→ Issue API keys to partners/customers
→ Usage plans: throttle and quota per API key
→ "Partner A: 1000 requests/day, 10 req/second"
→ Use for: monetized APIs, partner integrations
```

### Throttling and Caching
```
THROTTLING:
Account level: 10,000 requests/second (soft limit, can increase)
Stage level: set per-stage limit
Method level: set per-method limit (override stage)
→ Exceeds limit → 429 Too Many Requests

CACHING:
→ Cache responses at API Gateway level (0.5 GB to 237 GB)
→ TTL: 0-3600 seconds (default: 300)
→ Cache key: URL + optionally headers/query strings
→ Invalidate: client can force cache refresh with Cache-Control: max-age=0
→ Cost: $0.02/hour for 0.5 GB cache (~$14.40/month)
→ Use for: read-heavy APIs with frequently repeated requests
```

---

blocks.content:
LAMBDA AUTHORIZER (custom auth):

→ Use for: end-user authentication

IAM AUTHORIZATION:

API KEYS + USAGE PLANS:
→ Issue API keys to partners/customers
→ Usage plans: throttle and quota per API key
→ "Partner A: 1000 requests/day, 10 req/second"
→ Use for: monetized APIs, partner integrations

Stage level: set per-stage limit

→ Exceeds limit → 429 Too Many Requests

CACHING:

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — API Gateway
sections.rawMarkdown:
### API Gateway Real-World Failure Flow

```
Client traffic arrives
        │
        ▼
 API Gateway accepts request
        │
   ┌────┼────────────────────────────┐
   ▼    ▼                            ▼
Throttle hit   auth/cache/stage mismatch   backend slow/error
   │            │                          │
   ▼            ▼                          ▼
 429s         wrong behavior / stale data  5xx / timeouts
   │            │                          │
   └────────────┴──────────────┬───────────┘
                               ▼
                       user sees broken API

SENIOR MENTAL MODEL:
Gateway incidents happen in layers.
The backend can be healthy while the API is still broken because of stage config,
authorizer cache, throttling, mapping, or upstream edge caching.
```

**Q: Clients are getting random 429 errors from API Gateway. How do you debug that in production?**
```
"I would first identify which throttle layer is firing.

API Gateway has multiple places where requests can be limited:
→ account-level regional throttle
→ stage-level throttle
→ method-level throttle
→ usage plan / API key quota
→ backend itself returning 429 or timing out

So I check:
→ CloudWatch metric: 4XXError and specifically 429 count
→ access logs with requestId, API key, route, status
→ whether one customer or route is noisy

Then I separate protection from capacity:
if 429 is intentional to protect backend, good.
If 429 is because limits are too low relative to normal traffic, then it is a config problem.

Senior answer includes both:
protect backend with throttling, but also shape traffic upstream and scale the backend so
normal business traffic is not treated like abuse."
```

**Q: When would you choose API Gateway over ALB for Lambda or container backends?**
```
"Choose API Gateway when the API product needs gateway features:
auth, throttling, quotas, request validation, usage plans, API keys, versioned stages.

Choose ALB when:
→ simple HTTP routing is enough
→ lower cost matters at high request volume
→ you want one front door for ECS/EC2 with optional Lambda targets

For internal microservices, ALB is often enough.
For public APIs, partner APIs, mobile backends, or APIs needing per-client governance,
API Gateway is usually the better fit.

The senior tradeoff is not 'which service can technically route traffic' but
'where do we want policy, throttling, auth, and product controls to live?'"
```

**Q: A backend deploy succeeded, but users still see old or broken API behavior. Where do you look?**
```
"I think in layers:
→ API Gateway stage deployment not promoted?
→ cached authorizer result still in use?
→ API Gateway response cache serving stale data?
→ CloudFront in front of API caching unexpectedly?
→ Lambda alias/version still pointing to old code?
→ canary stage got updated but production stage did not?

This is a classic senior troubleshooting question because the backend may be correct.
The issue is often stale control-plane state at the gateway or edge layer."
```

---

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
API Gateway → Lambda: most common serverless integration
API Gateway → ECS/ALB/HTTP backends: proxy to containers and services
API Gateway → Cognito/JWT authorizers/Lambda authorizers: auth layer
API Gateway → CloudWatch: access logs, execution logs, metrics
API Gateway → WAF: request filtering when attached
API Gateway ← CloudFront: optional edge caching and global acceleration
API Gateway ← Route 53: custom domain routing

NEXT: Observability — CloudWatch, CloudTrail, X-Ray
How you see, debug, and operate everything you just built.
```

---

blocks.content:
API Gateway → Lambda: most common serverless integration
API Gateway → ECS/ALB/HTTP backends: proxy to containers and services
API Gateway → Cognito/JWT authorizers/Lambda authorizers: auth layer
API Gateway → CloudWatch: access logs, execution logs, metrics
API Gateway → WAF: request filtering when attached
API Gateway ← CloudFront: optional edge caching and global acceleration
API Gateway ← Route 53: custom domain routing

NEXT: Observability — CloudWatch, CloudTrail, X-Ray
How you see, debug, and operate everything you just built.

revisionPoints: API Gateway → Lambda: most common serverless integration
